description.summary: Animated anthropomorphic being created from inanimate matter (clay or mud) in Jewish folklore. Most famous is the Golem of Prague, created by Rabbi Judah Loew in the 16th century to defend the Jewish community.
description.full: The golem is an animated anthropomorphic being in Jewish folklore, created entirely from inanimate matter such as clay or mud. The most famous golem legend is the Golem of Prague, attributed to Rabbi Judah Loew ben Bezalel in the 16th century. According to the legend, the golem was formed from clay and brought to life through ritual use of sacred names of God, typically by inscribing the word 'emet' (truth) on its forehead or placing a tablet (shem) with God's name in its mouth. The golem is described as large, strong, mute, and obedient, lacking a soul and the ability to speak. Its purpose was typically to serve as a defender of the Jewish community, particularly the Prague ghetto, protecting Jews from persecution and pogroms. However, the legend warns that the golem could become uncontrollable, growing violent or following orders too literally. The creature could be deactivated by removing the animating shem or erasing the first letter of 'emet' to spell 'met' (death). The golem legend has roots in Jewish mysticism (Kabbalah) and the Genesis creation story, and has profoundly influenced later concepts of artificial life, including Frankenstein's monster and modern robotics ethics.

etymology.origin: From Hebrew 'גולם' (golem), meaning 'shapeless mass' or 'raw material.' Appears in Psalm 139:16 of the Hebrew Bible referring to an unformed substance.
etymology.meaning: Shapeless mass, unformed substance, raw material
etymology.first_use: Biblical era (in different context); folklore use medieval period onward

features.description: Typically depicted as very large and strong
Made entirely from clay or mud
Roughly humanoid but lacking refined features
'Emet' (truth) on forehead or shem tablet in mouth

physical.morphology: Humanoid figure made from clay. Large, strong, crude in appearance. Lacks soul, speech, and independent will.

abilities.description: Extremely strong, capable of great physical feats
Does not tire, can work continuously

patterns: May become uncontrollable if improperly managed
Interprets orders literally (can lead to problems)
limitations: Must be deactivated on Sabbath (some traditions)
Can become dangerous if orders unclear or loses control

range.description: Jewish folklore tradition, strongest association with Prague, Czech Republic. Concept appears in various Jewish communities across Europe and Middle East.

cultural_centers.name: Prague
cultural_centers.significance: Site of most famous golem legend - Rabbi Loew's Golem of Prague
cultural_centers.notes: Old-New Synagogue and Jewish Quarter associated with legend

first_recorded.source: Kabbalistic texts and Jewish mystical traditions

famous_legend.event: Golem of Prague created by Rabbi Judah Loew
famous_legend.notes: Legend codified in writing much later (19th century)

origins.cultural_roots: Jewish mysticism (Kabbalah) and the Genesis creation story where God forms Adam from dust. Early rabbinic texts discuss creation of life through sacred names and rituals.
origins.earliest_accounts: Talmudic references to creating life through mystical means. Medieval Kabbalistic texts describe golem creation rituals.
origins.folklore_connections: Connected to broader themes of artificial life, creation, and the relationship between creator and created.

timeline.significance: Rabbis discussing mystical creation of living beings
Sefer Yetzirah and other texts describe golem creation rituals
Most famous golem story - protection of Prague Jewish ghetto

timeline.event: First published account of Prague Golem

timeline.significance: Popular novel brings golem to international attention
Silent film classic spreading golem legend globally

history.modern_era: The golem remains powerful symbol in Jewish culture and broader discussions of artificial life, robotics, and creation ethics. Prague maintains golem tourism industry. Concept influences science fiction, AI ethics, and discussions of technology.

golem_of_prague.creator: Rabbi Judah Loew ben Bezalel (Maharal of Prague)
golem_of_prague.purpose: Defend Prague Jewish ghetto from antisemitic attacks and blood libels
golem_of_prague.creation_method: Formed from clay from Vltava River banks, animated through ritual and shem (sacred name)
golem_of_prague.activation: Word 'emet' (אמת - truth) inscribed on forehead or shem placed in mouth
golem_of_prague.deactivation: Remove shem or erase first letter of 'emet' to make 'met' (מת - death)
golem_of_prague.story: Rabbi Loew created golem to protect Jewish community. Golem performed tasks and protected against persecution. Eventually grew uncontrollable and violent. Rabbi deactivated golem on Sabbath eve. Body placed in attic of Old-New Synagogue where it allegedly remains.
golem_of_prague.variations: Different versions exist regarding golem's end - some say it became violent, others that it served peacefully until no longer needed.
golem_of_prague.historical_note: No contemporary accounts exist. Story emerged in oral tradition and written form centuries after Rabbi Loew's lifetime. Rabbi Loew was real historical figure (1512-1609), but golem creation is legend.

traditions: Use of sacred names to animate inanimate matter
Golem as protector of Jewish communities
folklore.beliefs: Golem represents human attempt to emulate divine creation, with inherent limitations and dangers. Serves as cautionary tale about creation and responsibility.

religious.sensitivity_note: Golem is part of Jewish religious and cultural tradition, particularly Kabbalistic mysticism. Respectful treatment of religious aspects is essential.
religious.respect_guidelines: Recognize Jewish cultural and religious context. Avoid trivialization of sacred concepts. Acknowledge distinction between folklore and religious practice.

literature: "Frankenstein" by Mary Shelley (1818) - influenced by golem concept
"The Golem and the Jinni" by Helene Wecker (2013)
"The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay" by Michael Chabon (2000)
film_tv: Simpsons Treehouse of Horror XVII (2006) - Golem parody
comics_games: Various superhero comics featuring golem characters
World of Warcraft and other fantasy games
influence: Modern robotics and AI ethics discussions

meanings: Human limitation in imitating divine creation

researchers.name: Gershom Scholem
researchers.contribution: Research on Jewish mysticism and golem traditions

researchers.name: Byron Sherwin
researchers.contribution: Studies on golem legend and Jewish ethics

organizations.name: Jewish Museum in Prague
organizations.focus: Preservation of golem legend and Jewish history

research.ongoing_studies: Contemporary research examines golem legend's origins, variations, and influence on modern concepts of artificial intelligence, robotics ethics, and creator responsibility.

sources.title: On the Kabbalah and Its Symbolism
The Golem Legend: Origins and Implications
The Golem Remembered, 1909-1980: Variations of a Jewish Legend
